utterances: Thank you very much for having me.
Bettina Luescher, chief spokesperson for the World Food Programme, with us from her office in New York.
Still with us, the deputy managing editor for Time Magazine, author of ""Is It Time to Invade Burma?,"" Romesh Ratnesar.
Let's get a caller on the line.
Karen joins us, Karen calling from Waterloo in Iowa.
Hi, Neal, thanks for taking the call.
Sure.
My point is that my son is just leaving on his second deployment to the Persian Gulf.
He was first in Afghanistan.
Where is any military effort going to come from?
We're short now.
Romesh Ratnesar, I think there are - well, more than a dozen U.S.
naval vessels in the area that are rushing to be in position, but other than that?
Well, as I understand it, I think Admiral Keating today said that there are at least a dozen, maybe more, helicopters available that could ferry in supplies to the delta.
You know, it would depend on what kind of operation we were talking about.
I mean, in the case of the tsunami, it involved about 15,000 American troops operating in different capacities.
I don't know what would be required in Burma, but there are assets available in the region that could within, I think, a moment's notice, could be deployed to help the people in this area.
But I think as in the tsunami case, you're talking about mostly naval and air-force personnel.
I think that's right.
I mean, I think some civil-affairs people would probably be needed if the government were to accept their presence.
I think, most likely, if you're going to get any cooperation from the Burmese government, I think they would probably agree to air and naval assets rather than troops on the ground.
I think what Karen is worried about is, what if the Burmese government doesn't agree?
What if...?
Well, yeah.
There is a difference between force and cooperation.
It resonates with Khaled Majid, a retired government official.
Imran Khan - the only personality in this country now.
He can shape the country.
Well, hopefully, again.
I think the - ideally don't want an American face on this, for lots of reasons.

If we can convince the other neighboring countries in the region, who all have an interest in averting a humanitarian disaster in Burma, to cooperate and to contribute their own assets, their own people.

I think that, ideally, would be better than - I agree, than sending thousands of American troops who we don't have to spare, and who would possibly create, in the minds of the Burmese government, more paranoia than they already have.

Yet when you conclude your piece, Romesh, by saying, ""As a response to the tsunami proved the world's capacity for mercy is limitless, but we still haven't figured out when to give war a chance,"" the idea that people think you're talking about force is hard to escape here.
I guess it depends on what you define as force.

I mean, if - any intervention without - that were to come without the permission of the Burmese government would be seen by the government and by the military as a hostile action, which is why I argue in the piece that this has to be thought through very carefully.
The consequences of doing something could be great in the amount of help you could provide to people who are in great risk.
On the other hand, you have to think about what the reaction would be, and in the long run, how you manage the fallout and the possible response from the Burmese authorities.
From a military mom's point of view, I am absolutely, thoroughly, totally for the assistance if it's accepted.
I don't want to propagate any kind of suffering in a country that is already filled with it.
However, we still have people in Kosovo.
We have people in Afghanistan.
We have people in the Gulf, Navy, and we have people on the ground, not only National Guard, Marines and Army, but also in the other forces.
And I just have to say, there is a difference between cooperation and force.
And when it comes to force, you really are talking about putting a whole different face on the issue.
Thank you so much.
And thank you for the call, Karen.
Let's go now to Peter, Peter with us from Fort Lauderdale.
Hello.
Hi.
Go ahead, please.
HI.

Unfortunately, I have a very pessimistic view of the situation, because in light of the high priority that the military junta in Myanmar gave to their own election this past weekend, over the ongoing emergency operations...
A constitutional referendum that would allow them to stay in power, yes.
Yes.

And in light of the fact that the generals would stamp their own individual names on some of the deliveries, supplies, for the purposes of media and PR and whatnot, it's very clear to me that the reason why even these nonpolitical operations that are simply involved with delivering food and medical supplies are being denied is because the junta is strictly concerned with their own self-preservation.

And they're concerned with negative PR, and they don't want the world to see, or perhaps even more importantly, they don't want their own local population to see that the junta is incapable, and quite honestly, unwilling, to take the necessary steps to help its own population.
So you're arguing bring in the aid at gunpoint?
Absolutely.
And not a U.S.
force, because I agree that the U.S.
military is currently strapped, unfortunately, I might add, but I would suggest a U.N.
- you know, a world-cooperative U.N.
force for maximum effectiveness at this point.

Because I don't see any other option for the hundreds of thousands, perhaps millions, of people that are at risk in this country, where their own government is not willing to allow outside help strictly for the fear of negative PR, it seems.
I wonder, Romesh Ratnesar, I think broadly Peter agrees with some of your points, but nevertheless, the prospect of - if this goes wrong, first of all, it would take a long time to get the U.N.
to set up such a force, and staff it, and arrange it, and organize it, and deliver it.
Nevertheless, even if that were possible, in time enough to intervene in a good fashion, things go wrong.
What if the Burmese started shooting at these aircraft?
Well, you're right.
I mean, there are risks in mounting any operation along these lines.
Any humanitarian intervention can go wrong, even those mounted with the best of intentions.

The case in point for the United States is the 1993 mission to Somalia, which we began as a humanitarian mission to deliver food aid, and ended up embroiling the United States in guerilla combat in Mogadishu.
I agree with you.
I mean, there are huge risks involved in doing anything like this.
And I think an intervention should not be undertaken lightly, which is why I think there are steps that the world can take, short of military action, that have not been tried.
The Security Council could...
What role does China have to play in this?

Because I know that China is somewhat considered an ally of the Burmese government, and I wonder if the Chinese have been persuaded to put very strong pressure from their side of this on the Burmese government to allow things to go forward.
I think that's the first step.

I think the Chinese have the most influence, of any of the actors in this, over the junta, and I think the first step would be to try to get the Chinese to use their influence to convince the Burmese that they need to accept the outside help.
If that doesn't happen, I think there are some other steps that the Security Council could take, such as freezing assets, or even threatening the arrest of individual members of the regime.
But I think military action, there's no question, because of the risks involved, has to be a last resort.
Peter, thanks very much for the call.
You're welcome.
We're talking with Romesh Ratnesar, the deputy managing editor of Time Magazine, about his op-ed, ""Is It Time to Invade Burma?"" You're listening to Talk of the Nation from NPR News.
And joining us now is Ko Pyi, a political activist from Myanmar, who fled here to the U.S.
seven years ago as a result of persecution.
He has a family in the city of Rangoon, which was hit by Cyclone Nargis, and joins us now from his office in Anaheim, California.
Nice to have you with us today.
Thanks for having me, Neal.
(SOUNDBITE OF SIRENS)

And as you're reading and hearing the news about the reluctance of the government in Myanmar to accept aid or accept logistical help and support being offered by other countries in the world, do you think that there's a point to air-dropping supplies if the government does not accept it?
It is - it is necessary for the international community to do whatever it takes to be - for the help to be able to reach to the people of Burma.
The people of Burma are in desperate need of help in any form and they will welcome help from - in any form.
They will welcome help in any form, even if it's forced through, against the government's wishes?
That is correct.
First of all, let me stress the fact that the Burma - the government of Burma is not a legitimate government.

They came to power by killing thousands of people in 1988, and the international community actually does not really even have to be asking for permission or working with the government of the Burma, because it's not a legitimate government, and you know, if they do whatever it takes to be able to reach out to Burma, the people of Burma will be forever grateful.
The - some of the difficulties we've talked about at this point, but nevertheless, your urgency, then, is for the deliveries of supplies as quickly as possible, by hook or by crook.
That is correct.
And I wonder, have you been able to reach your family members?
Not at all, not at all.
And I've been worried sick.
Ever since the cyclone knocked out all the phone lines and Internet connections, I haven't been able to reach any of my family or friends back in Burma.
And have you been able to find - so you don't know what happened to them?
I don't know, but my wife was able to reach her family back in Burma.
And are they OK?

They are OK physically, but - meaning that they did not sustain any personal injury, but they had extensive damage to their building, and the whole - the entire roof was blown off by the wind, and they had to move in with one of the family members.
And at this point, I presume, you could not get a visa to return to your country?
